emails.subject_line: something we noticed at pratt regional
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

PRMC just turned a profitable quarter after nearly a decade of losses, and the county pulled back its lease termination notice. That reversal signals real momentum.

Most rural critical access hospitals in that position still carry $11M+ in aging AR. If that backlog persists, it offsets every operational gain you've made.

We work with CFOs at similarly-sized sole community providers to recover revenue from accounts 90+ days past due without adding headcount.

Curious whether PRMC is prioritizing AR recovery or cost containment right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought on your path forward, alan
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

Your press quote on PRMC's stabilization plan stood out. As CFO navigating a decade of losses while managing Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement compression, the margin for error is near zero.

Leaders in your seat typically face a compounding problem: the staff capacity to chase denials and aging AR shrinks right when the volume matters most.

We've helped CFOs recover 15-20% of write-off-ready AR with no upfront cost.

Is reducing that $11M AR backlog something you're actively working on this quarter?

emails.subject_line: what other rural hospital cfos are doing
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

Other CFOs at sole community providers in the Midwest facing similar reimbursement pressure have shifted AR recovery from a staffing problem to a technology problem. The ones seeing results are capturing 20-30% more on accounts they had essentially written off.

With PRMC's hiring freeze still in place, adding coding or billing staff isn't viable. But autonomous AR recovery doesn't require headcount.

Would it help to see how a comparable rural hospital approached this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at pratt regional
emails.email_body:
Hi Alan,

PRMC has $11M+ in AR more than 90 days past due over the last two years. For a 35-bed hospital running on thin margins after a decade of losses, that backlog is material.

Decreased Medicare and Medicaid reimbursement rates plus low utilization makes revenue cycle efficiency the one lever that doesn't require volume growth.

We work with CFOs at similar-sized facilities on exactly this challenge.

Curious whether PRMC is prioritizing AR recovery or reducing denials at the front end?

emails.subject_line: digging deeper into pratt regional's ar challenge
emails.email_body:
Hey Alan,

The $11M AR backlog at PRMC compounds in a specific way: accounts past 90 days have recovery rates that drop 30-40% each additional month. At current Medicare/Medicaid reimbursement rates, write-offs accelerate faster than new revenue can offset them.

Facilities your size with a hiring freeze can't add billing staff. The math only works if recovery becomes more efficient per claim, not per person.

Is this kind of AR aging curve something your team is actively modeling?

emails.subject_line: a specific recommendation for prmc
emails.email_body:
Hello Alan,

For a sole community provider at PRMC's scale, the highest-ROI move is autonomous coding and claim re-processing on the 90+ day bucket. Facilities similar to yours have recovered 15-25% of accounts that were queued for write-off, without adding staff.

Given PRMC's profitable last quarter and the county's renewed commitment, this is the window to convert AR momentum into sustained margin.

Would it help to see how similar organizations approached this specifically?

emails.subject_line: april's coding throughput at johns hopkins
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare operations. Your work bridging quality and operational excellence at Hopkins is clear.

Hospitals at Hopkins' scale typically have coding throughput gaps that sit invisible inside otherwise strong operational metrics. The larger the system, the harder it is to see where revenue cycle velocity is slowing.

We work with COOs at academic medical centers to surface exactly these gaps.

Curious whether Hopkins is prioritizing coding accuracy or throughput speed right now?

emails.subject_line: april's revenue cycle velocity at jhmi
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Your ACHE service recognition and EIR role at Pitt Public Health signal a leader focused on system-level change, not just operational maintenance.

At AMCs like Hopkins, coding backlogs quietly compound. A 5% denial rate on inpatient volume at your scale represents tens of millions annually. Most COOs don't see it until quarter-end.

We've helped similar organizations reduce denials by 20%+ and cut coding lag by 30%.

Is coding capacity something your revenue cycle team is actively measuring?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins' revenue cycle gap
emails.email_body:
Hello April,

Academic medical centers consistently leave revenue on the table in professional coding due to volume and complexity. Hopkins runs one of the highest-acuity patient mixes in the country.

We have a breakdown of how comparable AMCs closed their coding gaps without adding FTEs. Given your operational focus and what you've built at Hopkins, I think it maps directly to your situation.

Would it help to see how similar organizations approached this?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + coding capacity
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare leadership. Your operational lens at Hopkins is evident.

Academic medical centers at Hopkins' scale face a structural coding capacity problem: patient complexity outpaces coder bandwidth, and denials compound silently. High-acuity case mix means every missed code has outsized revenue impact.

We work with COOs at similar AMCs to close that gap without adding headcount.

Curious whether Hopkins is prioritizing coding accuracy or throughput capacity right now?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + staffing gaps in revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Your ACHE award and EIR role signal a systems thinker. Most COOs at AMCs don't discover their coding staffing gap until it shows up as a denial trend, by then it's 60-90 days behind.

We've documented that AMCs similar to Hopkins recover 15-20% more on complex inpatient claims when coding throughput is automated at the back end.

Is revenue cycle staffing coverage something your team is actively tracking right now?

emails.subject_line: johns hopkins + claim lag
emails.email_body:
Hello April,

High-complexity AMCs routinely lose 3-5% of net revenue to claim lag and coding backlog, not denial management. It's the quieter leak.

We've worked with COOs at comparable systems to cut coding lag by 30% and improve clean claim rates measurably. Given what you've built operationally at Hopkins, this maps directly to your priorities.

Would it help to see how similar organizations closed this specific gap?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at metrohealth
emails.email_body:
Hi John,

Read your post about MetroHealth's Trauma System 101 Community Town Hall. The outreach work at MetroHealth reflects a system investing in community trust alongside clinical excellence.

MetroHealth is projecting a $31.2M budget shortfall in 2026, down from $48.3M in 2025. That improvement is real progress, but $446M in charity care puts sustained pressure on revenue cycle margins.

We work with health systems facing that exact tension between mission and margin.

Would it make sense to explore how others have navigated this?

emails.subject_line: a thought on metrohealth's margin path
emails.email_body:
Hey John,

MetroHealth's charity care costs have doubled since 2022, now exceeding $1M per day. With county subsidies declining and Medicaid uncertainty growing, the margin on insured patient revenue has to carry more weight.

We've seen health systems in comparable positions recover 15-20% more on professional claims by addressing coding throughput, without adding billing staff.

If this resonates, happy to share more on what that looked like for a similar-size system.

emails.subject_line: metrohealth's revenue cycle and what's next
emails.email_body:
Hello John,

MetroHealth is navigating $50M in 2024 operating losses while investing in Magnet-level nursing, ED renovations, and new outpatient expansion. That's a system under financial strain while simultaneously growing.

Systems in that position need revenue cycle efficiency to scale without proportional cost increases. We've mapped exactly this dynamic for organizations like MetroHealth.

No worries either way, but figured it was worth flagging given what's on your plate in 2026.

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at metrohealth
emails.email_body:
Hi John,

Read your post about MetroHealth's Trauma System 101 Community Town Hall. The community trust MetroHealth is building sits alongside a $31.2M projected shortfall in 2026.

Most health systems in this position underestimate how much revenue cycle inefficiency is amplifying the gap. $446M in charity care compresses margin on every covered claim you do collect.

We've built a framework for health systems navigating this specific tension.

Worth sending over our revenue cycle efficiency benchmark report for safety-net systems?

emails.subject_line: a framework for metrohealth's margin challenge
emails.email_body:
Hey John,

MetroHealth's charity care costs doubled since 2022. With county subsidies declining from $35M to $32M in 2026, insured revenue cycle performance carries the entire margin story.

We put together a revenue recovery playbook specifically for public/safety-net health systems managing $400M+ in uncompensated care. It covers coding automation, denial patterns, and AR velocity benchmarks.

We put together this playbook for health systems like MetroHealth. Want a copy?

emails.subject_line: what a comparable system did with this
emails.email_body:
Hello John,

A safety-net health system with a comparable charity care burden used our AR recovery breakdown to identify $8M in recoverable revenue they had essentially written off. No new staff, no new contracts.

Given MetroHealth's 2026 budget constraints and the $31.2M shortfall, that kind of recovery changes the margin math meaningfully.

Happy to share the breakdown we used with that peer organization.

You've got an $800M Independence Health integration coming, new Epic rollout, plus capital projects across 25 hospitals. When a system grows that fast, revenue cycle ops can quietly lag behind the expansion.

We work with integration leads on exactly this kind of scaling challenge.

Curious whether WVU Medicine is prioritizing coding standardization or AR consolidation heading into the Independence integration?

emails.subject_line: a quick thought on the independence integration
emails.email_body:
Hey Carrie,

Your Women for Madison Summit talk and your EVP role in System Integration both signal someone who thinks about the operational seams, not just the headline numbers.

Here's what we've seen go wrong in comparable integrations: coding workflows don't get standardized early, and by month six you've got two revenue cycle systems producing different outcomes on the same claim types.

We've helped integration teams get ahead of this before Epic go-live makes it harder to fix.

Is coding standardization across the Independence hospitals something you're actively planning for?

emails.subject_line: before the independence integration closes
emails.email_body:
Hello Carrie,

WVU Medicine is planning $3B in capital projects over the next decade while absorbing five new hospitals in Western Pennsylvania. That's a lot of moving parts for revenue cycle to keep up with.

Systems that get ahead of coding and AR standardization before integration day recover significantly more in the first year. The ones that wait spend 12-18 months cleaning up avoidable variance.

Would it help to see how similar organizations handled this during a comparable integration?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed at wvu medicine

Health systems absorbing 5 new hospitals typically see a 12-18% increase in coding variance and a 20-25% spike in claim denials in the first year post-integration, particularly before Epic standardization is complete. At WVU Medicine's scale, that variance translates to $50-80M in delayed or lost revenue annually.

Curious whether WVU Medicine is prioritizing coding standardization or AR consolidation as the primary focus heading into this integration?

emails.email_body:

Your background in System Integration and your Women for Madison Summit speaking role both reflect a leader focused on the operational details that determine whether integrations actually deliver their projected value.

In comparable health system integrations of 4-6 hospitals, the revenue cycle gap between projected and actual performance in year one averages 8-12% of net revenue. For WVU Medicine's $6.7B system absorbing Independence, that's a $100M+ exposure range before any synergies materialize.

We've helped integration teams address coding workflow standardization and AR velocity alignment before Epic go-live, which is the window where intervention costs the least.

Is revenue cycle integration planning something your team is actively scoping for the Independence hospitals?

emails.subject_line: the specific window to act on this
emails.email_body:
Hello Carrie,

WVU Medicine has the most complex integration profile we've seen in a regional health system: 25 existing hospitals, 5 incoming hospitals, $800M capital commitment, new Epic rollout, and $3B in planned projects over the next decade. Each of those variables adds coding complexity.

Systems that standardize professional coding workflows before Epic go-live recover 15-20% more in year one compared to those that address it post-integration. The pre-go-live window is the highest-leverage point, and WVU Medicine is in it right now.

Systems in comparable positions have recovered 15-20% more on complex claims through coding throughput improvements, without adding staff or changing payer contracts.

Is revenue cycle efficiency something MetroHealth is actively measuring against a benchmark right now?

emails.subject_line: how comparable systems have handled this
emails.email_body:
Hello John,

Safety-net health systems with $400M+ in charity care and declining subsidies have recovered $8-12M annually from revenue cycle throughput improvements alone. No new FTEs, no new contracts.

MetroHealth's 2026 profile maps closely to those organizations, particularly given the post-layoff constraint and ongoing capital investment.

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about Baptist Memorial's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hi Nirmit,

Read your post about connecting the dots in healthcare leadership - your conversation with Andrew Baughen touched on the operational complexity physicians face as they move into administrative roles.

That complexity often shows up in revenue cycle too. At large academic medical centers like Baptist Memorial Memphis, physician documentation gaps and coding inconsistencies quietly drain 8-12% of net revenue before anyone flags it.

We work with systems that have reduced those gaps by 35-40% within two quarters by targeting the highest-value service lines first - orthopedics, cardiology, and complex surgical cases.

Curious whether Baptist Memorial is prioritizing physician documentation improvement or denial prevention right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought on Baptist Memorial's coding accuracy
emails.email_body:
Hey Nirmit,

Following up on my note last week. You're speaking at Becker's this April on operational transformation - a topic that intersects directly with revenue performance.

One comparison worth sharing: two similar-sized regional health systems in the Mid-South both ran manual coding workflows. The one that implemented AI-assisted coding review cut its denial rate from 14% to under 5% in six months, recovering $3.2M annually. The other is still processing the same backlogs.

The difference wasn't budget - it was prioritization of the revenue cycle as a clinical quality issue, not just a back-office function.

Is improving first-pass claim rates something your team is actively working on heading into FY2026?
